region_of_origin: South India; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Uppuma; mapped to semolina-based dish as the closest defensible FoodOn class. Uppuma is a cooked semolina porridge with vegetables and spices.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Uppuma entry; A0CKP (cooked cereal dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a semolina-based cooked grain dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Uppuma in the clinical terminology system; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Uppuma is approximately 200 g (one plate or bowl), as typically served at a South Indian breakfast in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Uppuma — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Uppuma (cr_id F05050020). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for a comparable semolina upma preparation, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Uppuma exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated using semolina (durum wheat semolina, cooked) as the closest analogous food, which has a published GI of approximately 55–66 (mean ~65) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 33.62) / 100 ≈ 21.9 for a 200 g serving (16.81 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 348 mg per 100 g with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Uppuma portion size to 100–150 g and reduce added salt during preparation, as the 348 mg sodium per 100 g contributes meaningfully to the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target; adding potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or peas can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined semolina with medium GI (~65) and low dietary fibre (1.95 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Uppuma in modest portions (100–150 g), pair it with a protein source such as dal or eggs to blunt the post-meal glucose response, and consider substituting whole wheat semolina (atta rava) to increase fibre and reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat (9.22 g per 100 g) with borderline sodium and low fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, Uppuma prepared with vegetable oil rather than ghee keeps saturated fat below 2 g per 100 g, but the 348 mg sodium per 100 g warrants portion control and reduced salt in preparation to stay within heart-healthy sodium limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, curry leaves) with low saturated fat when oil-prepared
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Uppuma is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when prepared with vegetable oil rather than ghee, as ginger and curry leaves provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory compounds (gingerols, polyphenols); those sensitive to nightshades should reduce or omit green chilli and tomato.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Uppuma (also spelled Upma) is a popular South Indian savoury breakfast dish widely consumed across India and in Singapore's Indian community. It is made by dry-roasting semolina (rava or sooji — coarse-ground durum wheat) until lightly golden, then cooking it with a tempering of mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chilli, ginger, and onion in vegetable oil or ghee, with water added to form a soft, thick porridge-like consistency. Vegetables such as peas, carrots, and beans are commonly added. The texture is soft and slightly grainy, with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile. Per 100 g, Uppuma provides 164 kcal, 3.46 g protein, 9.22 g fat, 16.81 g carbohydrate, 1.95 g dietary fibre, and 348 mg sodium. The dish has a medium glycaemic index (~65) due to refined semolina, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is borderline elevated, relevant for hypertension management. The anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, curry leaves) offer modest benefits for inflammatory conditions. Uppuma is vegetarian and typically halal. It is a culturally significant breakfast item in Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and among the South Indian diaspora in Singapore, often served with coconut chutney or sambar.
common_user_queries: Is Uppuma suitable for high blood pressure?
Can I eat Upma if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact Uppuma entry (cr_id F05050020). Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN for a comparable preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked semolina as the reference food, which is the closest defensible approved-source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for a comparable semolina upma preparation, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for Uppuma as prepared in Singapore are not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN at the level of granularity required; these fields require a dedicated laboratory analysis or a more granular recipe-level calculation to populate. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed negligible/absent for a vegetable-oil or ghee preparation of Uppuma; no industrial partially hydrogenated oils are used in traditional preparation). 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on cooked semolina (durum wheat) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for Uppuma exists in the approved GI source database. 5. is_vegan set to false because ghee (clarified butter) is a common preparation fat; oil-prepared versions would be vegan. 6. is_dairy_free set to false for the same reason; oil-only preparations are dairy-free. 7. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry for Uppuma exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of the processing date.
